sources.summary: G3 is included as a Cartwheel-group companion with one ULX and a possible nuclear source, with unresolved X-ray emission modeled jointly with G2.

quotes.quote: Here, we address the study of a population of ULXs in the Cartwheel Galaxy -- the archetypal collisional ring galaxy -- and in the companion galaxies G1, G2, G3 that together form a compact group of 4 members SGC 0035-3357; Iovino2002 . Source P32, associated with G3, was listed in Appendix A in Wolter2004 . In summary, the total number of sources positionally associated to the group galaxies, for which we can investigate variability, are 37 in total: Cartwheel has 29 ULXs; G1 has 7 ULXs (plus 1 possibly nuclear source); G2 has only 1 possibly nuclear source; G3 has 1 ULX (plus 1 possibly nuclear source). We detect 44 sources, 29 of which positionally associated to the Cartwheel galaxy (plus two with non-significant flux detections), 8 to G1 (of which 1 possibly linked to the nucleus), 1 to G2 (possibly linked to the nucleus) and 2 to G3 (of which 1 possibly linked to the nucleus).

We repeat the same procedure for G2 and G3. For these galaxies, we have not enough statistics to use complex models and indeed to fit more than one parameter, so we fixed the n_ H at 1.2 10 ^ 21 cm ^ 2 , the value derived from the average spectrum of the point-like sources, while we leave the power-law index free. The best-fitting power-law index and the unabsorbed luminosity for G2 and G3 are indicated in Table tab:fit_g1_g2_g3 . From the same table we can see that the non-resolved emission constitutes 64 and 56 per cent of the total emission, respectively for G2 and G3.

gmw_00003848.name: 3C343.1
gmw_00003848.overview: The strong radio source 3C343.1 is a close galaxy-quasar pair consisting of a galaxy and a quasar separated by no more than about 0.25 arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/0401007]. The galaxy, which has a redshift of z=0.344, is physically connected to the z=0.750 quasar by a radio bridge [arXiv:astro-ph/0401007].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C343.1 as a close galaxy-QSO radio pair, with a z=0.344 galaxy connected by a radio bridge to a z=0.750 QSO.

quotes.quote: The strong radio source 3C343.1 consists of a galaxy and a QSO separated by no more than about 0.25^ . The chance of this being an accidental superposition is conservatively 1 10^ 8 . The z=0.344 galaxy is connected to the z=0.750 QSO by a radio bridge. The numerical relation between the two redshifts is that predicted from previous associations. This pair is an extreme example of many similar physical associations of QSOs and galaxies with very different redshifts.

gmw_00003849.name: J 123800.91+621336.0
gmw_00003849.overview: J 123800.91+621336.0 is a quasar at a redshift of z=0.44 that is classified as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 analog [arXiv:1110.5341]. It exhibits repeated X-ray variability, a steep X-ray spectrum, weak [O III] emission, and an excess of ionized iron emission [arXiv:1110.5341].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1238 as a z=0.44 NLS1-like quasar in the Chandra Deep Field-North with repeated X-ray variability, weak [O 3], strong Fe emission, and steep spectra.

quotes.quote: Six of the 17 light curves that the KS test flags as variable belong to a single source, J 123800.91+621336.0 (hereafter ``J1238'') at z=0.44 with absolute i- magnitude M_i = -23.04 . This source was observed many times as it falls in the Chandra Deep Field-North e.g., abbghsv03 .

Figure j1238Fig shows the 4000--5200 \ region of the SDSS spectrum of J1238 (in black) with the quasar composite of v+01 overplotted in red for comparison. The FWHM of the H _ and Mg 2 2800 (not shown) lines is 1860 km s ^ 1 , while the [ O 3 ] 5007 line is quite weak in comparison to the composite value. J1238 also shows an excess of ionized iron emission in the 4500--4600 \ region. Fitting a Galactic-absorbed power law to the observed-frame 0.5--8 keV region, we find that the spectrum is steeper than typical quasars, with 2.2 < < 2.4 in most epochs (Figure gammaJ1238Fig ).
All of these traits indicate that J1238 is a quasar analog of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) population see, e.g., reviews in pogge00, komossa07 .

gmw_00003850.name: NGC 4192
gmw_00003850.overview: NGC 4192 is a massive, edge-on spiral galaxy in the Virgo Cluster [arXiv:0710.3867, arXiv:1208.2845] that hosts a warped neutral hydrogen disk [arXiv:1208.2845, arXiv:1206.6916] and a compact nuclear radio and X-ray source [arXiv:2202.13225]. It exhibits extraplanar X-ray emission [arXiv:2312.04050], a vertically extended radio continuum halo [arXiv:1304.1279], and nearby hydrogen clouds that appear to stream from its disk [arXiv:0707.3278, arXiv:0802.2100].

sources.summary: NGC 4192 is discussed as a massive edge-on spiral and plausible interloper whose observed velocity offset from NGC 4254 could be reconciled by the Virgo cluster tidal field.

quotes.quote: A massive spiral is in fact present near this position: NGC 4192 (M 98). This galaxy, which is seen close to edge-on, has a maximum rotation velocity corrected for inclination of 236 km s ^ 1 , significantly higher than that of NGC 4254 (193 km s ^ 1 , according to the HyperLeda database), making it compatible with the 1.5:1 mass ratio used in our simulation. In our model, the interloper is today approaching us with a radial velocity of 715 km s ^ 1 w..t. the target spiral. In the real Universe, NGC 4192 is indeed approaching, but with a relative velocity of 2000 km s ^ 1 with respect to NGC 4254, i.e. much larger than in our model.

This additionnal velocity difference would account for the observed relative velocities of NGC 4192/4254, and goes in the right direction if NGC 4254 lies behind the Virgo Cluster center. NGC 4192 is thus a fully possible interloper, in spite of its large distance and relative velocity.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4192 as an edge-on Virgo spiral with a warped H i disk, western radio extension, and disk-parallel inner polarization vectors.

quotes.quote: NGC 4192 NGC 4192 is an SABb edge-on galaxy located in the northwestern outskirts of the cluster, about 4 8 (1.44 Mpc in the sky plane) from M 87 (Virgo A). The galaxy shows a warped H i disk (Cayatte et al. ). The total power peak almost coincides with the optical position of the galactic center (Fig. 4192tp ).

On the western side of the galaxy disk, the total power extension can be seen. As checked using the NVSS data, this is not associated with point background sources. The polarization B-vectors are oriented roughly parallel to the disk in its inner parts.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4192 as one of three Virgo edge-on late-type galaxies with significant extraplanar X-ray emission and corrects its corona luminosity for ACIS coverage.

quotes.quote: Somewhat surprisingly, only three galaxies (NGC 4192, NGC 4216 and NGC 4388) are found to have S/N > 3 , indicating significant extraplanar X-ray emission. To our knowledge, this is the first report of significant extended X-ray emission from NGC 4192 and NGC 4216, although the S/N is only moderate ( 4 ).

Unfortunately, the limited S/N of these three galaxies does not permit a meaningful X-ray spectral analysis for the extraplanar hot gas, for which we also adopt the above photon flux-to-luminosity conversion. In the case of NGC 4192, whose corona region slightly exceeds the ACIS FoV, we have assumed a spatially uniform corona surface brightness to correct for the derived X-ray hot gas corona luminosity. The optical image, the X-ray flux map, and the X-ray intensity profile along the minor axis of these three galaxies are shown in Figure fig:image .
They are in fact the three galaxies with the highest SFR, and two of them (NGC 4192 and NGC 4216) have the highest stellar mass among the Virgo sample.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4192, alias M 98, as a fast-moving Virgo galaxy with nearby H I clouds and possible streaming gas, potentially tied to NGC 4254 harassment.

quotes.quote: NGC 4192 = M 98 is a galaxy located in the NW periphery of the Virgo cluster, with a negative heliocentric velocity (-142 ) indicative of large relative motion with respect to the cluster itself. A number of HI clouds with velocities between 60 and 100 are found in its vicinity, spectrally well separated from Milky Way emission. They would typically be cataloged as High (or Intermediate) Velocity Clouds, perigalactic (or Local Group) dwellers, and most of them may very well be just that. Some of the clouds, however, appear to stream out of M 98, stretching over more than 1^ , well matched both spatially and kinematically to the disk of M 98. The characteristics of this system are under close scrutiny.

sources.summary: ALFALFA reports HI clouds apparently streaming from M 98 and notes it may have caused the harassment that produced the VirgoHI21 stream near NGC 4254.

quotes.quote: The authors of the simulation speculate that, given its location and velocity, the culprit could be M 98 = NGC 4192. ALFALFA finds an extended HI appendage apparently emanating from that galaxy. Some of the clouds, however, appear to stream out of M 98, stretching over more than 1^ , well matched both spatially and kinematically to the disk of M 98. The characteristics of this system are under close scrutiny. Of particular interest is the fact that suggest that M 98 may be the culprit responsible for the harassment of NGC 4254, observed in the form of the stream VirgoHI21 is a part of.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4192 has symmetric radio continuum emission, a steep disk spectral index, a 6 cm radio halo, asymmetric 20 cm polarization, and an X-shaped regular magnetic field.

quotes.quote: NGC 4192 The overall surface brightness of the radio continuum disk of this highly inclined galaxy is low ( 300 Jy/beam at 6 cm) as the star formation activity (H ). The 20 and 6 cm radio continuum emission distributions are symmetric. The spectral index is steep ( < -1 ) all over the disk. A vertically extended radio continuum halo is observed at 6 cm. Polarized emission at 20 cm is only detected along the minor axis to the southwest.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4192 as a new compact nuclear-source and LLAGN candidate, detected from radio cross-correlation and also associated with an X-ray source at the nucleus.

quotes.quote: The two nuclear sources that were not previously identified as AGN candidates are NGC 4192 and NGC 3877, both of which were identified as `nuclear' in Table t:new_AGNs from radio data alone The third source in that table, NGC 5792, had no X-ray data and so does not appear in Table t:cross_LCX . . As indicated in Sect ss:cross_L_C , this is the first evidence for a compact nuclear source in these two galaxies.

Three new nuclear sources have been identified from the radio cross-correlation: NGC 3877, NGC 4192, and NGC 5792 (Table t:new_AGNs ). NGC 3877 and NGC 4192 also have X-ray sources right at the nucleus. When compared to previous observations, we find that NGC 3877 is also variable. As suggested in Sect. ss:cross_L_C , follow-up data would help to confirm the nature of these apparent LLAGNs.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M 98 as a large Virgo spiral whose warped H I distribution spans about 15 arcmin, deriving an H I mass despite Galactic contamination.

quotes.quote: HIPASS J1213+14 : Cayatte et al. (1990) report on VLA \ observations of the large spiral galaxy M 98 (NGC 4192). They find the gas distribution to be warped, extending nearly 15 arcmin in diameter over a velocity range from about --360 to +120 \ (see also Chung et al. 2009). Assuming a distance of 16 Mpc, we derive an \ mass of 4.6 10^9 . Our HIPASS spectrum is highly contaminated by bright Galactic \ emission in the velocity range from about --100 to +60 \ as well as HVCs at higher velocities.

sources.summary: The paper groups NGC 4192 among Virgo galaxies affected by strong-field contamination and reports a steep C-band spectral index with high uncertainties in its inner disk.

quotes.quote: Three Virgo galaxies: NGC 4192, NGC 4388 and NGC 4438 These galaxies are highly affected by contamination from strong sources in the field, such as M87, rendering higher than normal rms noise and artifacts hard to clean out. This may have an effect on both spectral index results and polarization. We note that the C-band spectral index is steep in the inner disk for NGC 4192 (a rather weak source at the low end of our flux density cutoff for the survey), and that the uncertainties are high.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4192 as an exception among anemic Virgo galaxies, noting its edge-on orientation and low H i deficiency may indicate misclassification.

quotes.quote: Nevertheless, we observe that 12 out of 15 anemic galaxies lie on radial orbits except (NGC 4192, NGC 4450, and NGC 4419). NGC 4192 is seen nearly edge-on. Therefore, it is difficult to estimate its arm inter-arm contrast. The low H i deficiency of NGC 4192 indicates that this galaxy might be misclassified as an anemic galaxy.

gmw_00003851.name: 3C 17
gmw_00003851.overview: The elliptical galaxy 3C 17 is a broad-line radio galaxy [arXiv:0901.4718, arXiv:1710.02194] that serves as the brightest cluster galaxy in its environment [arXiv:1908.05229]. It features a curved, one-sided radio and X-ray jet [arXiv:0901.4718, arXiv:1908.05229], ionized gas extending 15 kiloparsecs northwest [arXiv:1903.10768], and an optical bridge connecting it to a southern companion galaxy [arXiv:1008.2683].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C17 as a powerful broad-line radio galaxy with asymmetric arcsecond emission and a one-sided parsec-scale VLBI jet aligned with its larger-scale jet.

quotes.quote: 0035 02 (3C17) This is the most powerful and most distant object in our sample. It is classified as a broad line radio galaxy (Tadhunter et al. 1993) and it is also characterised by optical polarisation (Tadhunter et al. 1997). High resolution 5 GHz VLA observations (Morganti et al. 1999) show that the source is very asymmetric on the arcsecond scale both in shape and brightness. The south-east arcsecond scale jet is aligned in position angle (p.a.) +100^ , and it is characterised by high brighness knots. It sharply bends by +200^ at 10 arcsec from the core.
No jet is visible on the north-western side, but only a diffuse lobe, with a ring-like structure, at 5 arcsec in projected distance form the core.

Our parsec scale image, given in Figure fig:3c17 , shows a one-sided morphology oriented in east-west at +100^ , roughly in agreement with the initial orientation of the south-east arcsecond scale jet.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the radio galaxy 3C 17, emphasizing its curved jet, X-ray and optical jet knots, broad-line nucleus, and ambiguous FRI/FRII radio morphology.

quotes.quote: We report on the X-ray emission from the radio jet of 3C 17 from observations and compare the X-ray emission with radio maps from the VLA archive and with the optical-IR archival images from the Hubble Space Telescope. X-ray detections of two knots in the 3C 17 jet are found and both of these features have optical counterparts. We derive the spectral energy distribution for the knots in the jet and give source parameters required for the various X-ray emission models, finding that both IC/CMB and synchrotron are viable to explain the high energy emission.

3C 17 is a radio galaxy ( z 0.22, Schmidt et al. 1965) with a peculiar radio structure investigated by Morganti et al. (1999). Its H emission has a strong broad component and both the [ O II ] 3727 and [ O III ] 5007 emission lines are extended (Dickson 1997).

3C 17 is a broad lined radio galaxy (BLRG) (Buttiglione et al. 2008) with a monochromatic radio luminosity, log P _ 1.4 GHz 26.9 which is at least two orders of magnitude above the division between FRI and FRII types (Ledlow Owen, 1996) but with an ambiguous radio morphology.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 17 as a broad-line radio galaxy with resolved jet and lobe emission, a 21 degree misalignment, curved jet structure, and precession indicators.

quotes.quote: Among the broad line radio galaxies, only 3C 17 and 3C 327.1, have high-quality radio maps with well resolved jet as well as lobe detections (Fig. fig:2JyBLRGs ). Though the lobe axis is now much more difficult to determine, because of the strong contrast in brightness between the likely Doppler boosted jets and the fainter parts of the lobes, the misalignment is evident in both cases. We estimate misalignment angles of 21 for 3C 17 and 35 for 3C 327.1, much more than the 5 for Cygnus A. Both sources show curved jets with a sharp bend at the brightest jet knot (hotspot), suggesting that the jets may be deflected there before hitting the lobe boundary a second time at the final hotspot.

These features are clear indicators for precession according to the criteria defined in Sect. sec:pcrits . Because the length scales are very similar to Cygnus A, the precession period must also be of the order of 10^6 yrs.

sources.summary: MUSE shows 3C 017 has broad nuclear Balmer emission, ionized gas extending northwest to 15 kpc, changing velocities, and a northern blueshifted knot.

quotes.quote: 3C 017: FR II/BLO, 1 = 3.58 kpc. Beside the bright central nuclear component, characterized by the presence of broad Balmer lines, the ionized gas extends in the NW direction out to 15 kpc. The gas velocity increases moving to the West up to 100 , but when turning toward the North it decreases to blueshifted velocities, down to 200 . In addition, a blueshifted compact emission line knot is found 20 kpc to the North. The velocity dispersion is, except on the nucleus, rather small.

sources.summary: The paper selects two knots in 3C 17's powerful radio-galaxy jet, gives its black-hole mass and redshift scale, and notes a possible IC/CMB contribution for S11.3.

quotes.quote: 3C 17: We select two knots (S3.7 and S11.3) in the powerful jet of the radio galaxy 3C 17. The mass of its SMBH is 5.0 10^ 8 Sikora2007 . The measured redshift of the host galaxy ( z = 0.22) corresponds to a conversion scale of 1 = 3.47 kpc. While we assume a synchrotron origin, we note that given the SED shape and unusual character of S11.3, it cannot be excluded that IC/CMB contributes to the emission from this knot Massaro2009,Rahman2023 . The radio, optical, and data are taken from Massaro2009 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0035-02 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This BLRG/FRII appears clearly disturbed in our optical image (Figure pks0035_online ). The radio galaxy shows a bridge linking with a companion galaxy 46 kpc to the South. We also detected a fan of _V =25.1 mag arcsec ^ 2 towards the NW. Heckman86 did not report the detection of any feature for this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper finds the host appears E-type but is reasonably fit with two components, with aperture photometry and literature classifications broadly supporting an elliptical interpretation.

quotes.quote: 3C 17 (Fig. 11.1) --- \ \ While the host galaxy appears to be E-type, the fit with two components works reasonably well. The aperture magnitude of the host inside 15 kpc from de Koff et al. (1996) agrees with our estimates to within the uncertainties. Based on ground-based and HST /NICMOS images, Zirbel (1996) and Donzelli et al. (2007) also classify this object as an elliptical galaxy. Floyd et al. (2008) and Donzelli et al. (2007) fit the H- band NICMOS image and find n 7.5 and 2.7 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 17 as a redshift 0.22 broad-line radio galaxy with a one-sided curved radio/X-ray jet and brightest-cluster-galaxy environment.

quotes.quote: The 4FGL catalog associated the source 4FGL J0038.7-0204 with the broad line radio galaxy (RDG) 3C 17 at redshift 0.22 as previously determined by in agreement with the SDSS spectrum we analyzed. The kiloparsec-scale radio morphology of 3C 17 is dominated by a single-sided, curved jet morganti1993radio also shining in the X-rays massaro2009jet,massaro2010chandra . Recent optical spectroscopic observations also revealed that 3C 17 is the brightest cluster galaxy in its large scale environment madrid20183c .

gmw_00003852.name: 3C 61.1
gmw_00003852.overview: The early-type galaxy 3C 61.1 [arXiv:1710.02194] is a very bright, complex giant radio galaxy [arXiv:1602.06624] with a double-lobed structure [arXiv:1810.11681, arXiv:1512.00014]. It is heavily obscured by dust [arXiv:1003.2467] and resides as the brightest member of a small galaxy group [arXiv:1003.2467, arXiv:0712.0595].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 61.1 as the brightest double-lobed radio galaxy in the North Celestial Pole field and discusses its flux, source model, and imperfect subtraction.

quotes.quote: The brightest source in our field ( >+80^ ) away is the double-lobed radio galaxy 3C 61.1, which has a flux density of 2 Jy at 4.7 GHz Hargrave1975 . The catalogue reports a flux density for 3C 61.1 of 970 80 mJy at 4.8 GHz, which is inconsistent with various measurements of 1.9 Jy at 5 GHz (the source is not thought to be significantly variable). Since they report 3 individual sources (they do not give the flux densities for these due to confusion of multiple sources hence the ``X"s in the catalogue entry) it may be that this is a typographical error in which they have reported one of the contributing sources.

The source 3C 61.1 has a peak brightness temperature of 9 mK above the background. In the middle panel of Fig. fig:cbassmap_sourcesub we show the C-BASS map after smoothing (and deconvolving) to the common 1^ FWHM resolution. The right panel shows the C-BASS map after subtracting the map of sources. Visual inspection shows that the subtraction has been successful, with most of the obvious sources no longer being visible. For the brightest few sources (3C 61.1, NGC 6251) the subtraction is not perfect.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C061.1 as a very bright, complex radio galaxy dominating the NCP field and limiting 21CMA calibration and deconvolution through grating-lobe residuals.

quotes.quote: The NCP sky at 50-200 MHz is actually dominated by two bright sources: a central bright quasar (NVSS J011732+892848) and a galaxy 3C61.1. 3C61.1 is a very bright giant galaxy with a flux density of 33 Jy at 150 MHz, which also shows a complex structure.

Indeed, we are still unable to perfectly CLEAN the sidelobes of the brightest radio galaxy, 3C061.1, located at 3^ .71 degrees from NCP. The complex structure of this source Lawrence96 is marginally resolved with 21CMA. Even with a better angular resolution and a multi-directional calibration using SAGECAL Yatawatta09,Kazemi11 , LOFAR imaging of the same source also contains significant errors Yatawatta13 . Our current experiment towards the detection of the EoR is thus largely limited by the presence of this very bright, complex radio galaxy in the NCP field.

sources.summary: The paper describes a faint-nucleus host with diffuse companions, a bulge plus off-center disturbed structure, and measurements consistent with an early-type galaxy.

quotes.quote: 3C 61.1 (Fig. 11.2) --- \ \ There are three diffuse companions. The nucleus appears much fainter than the host. The host galaxy is fit with a bulge and an off-center disturbed structure.

Donzelli et al. (2007) classify this object as an elliptical galaxy. The measured B/T=0.92 also reveals that the host is an early-type galaxy. Floyd et al. (2008) and Donzelli et al. (2007) estimate n 2.8 and 3.5, respectively, which agree well with our measurement.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 61.1 as an under-luminous dusty FR II radio-source host, probably the brightest member of a small group or larger cluster.

quotes.quote: 3C 61.1 is an FR II radio source in an under-luminous host galaxy ( M_H=-22.2 ), with apparent spiral arm structure (see paper II and references therein) in spite of its high radio power ( _ 10 L_ 178 =26.27 ). However, this source is the brightest member of a small group of galaxies visible on the NICMOS image, and is probably associated with a larger cluster that includes a radio-quiet AGN at z=0.184 kristian+78 some 30 to the east. It is clearly very dusty from the optical morphology dekoff+96 . The extinction map is complicated by the star formation in the apparent spiral arms, but the central region appears to be heavily obscured, and we estimate 0.5 H mag of extinction in the central region of this source (assuming a diffuse dust model after mathis90 ), giving an unextincted H band luminosity of M_H=-22.7 . It is likely to be the brightest cluster member (though this is uncertain without a proper study of the host galaxy of the nearby AGN).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 61.1 as the bright field-dominating resolved radio galaxy whose apparent low-level variability is attributed to calibration, extraction, and a basic model for a complex lobe-dominated source.

quotes.quote: This source is 3C 61.1, which dominates the field. While the result points towards low-level variability of 3C 61.1, the source is a well resolved radio galaxy 3C61 whose flux is dominated by 100-kpc-scale lobes, making it very unlikely that we would detect any intrinsic variability. It is more likely that this is the result of calibration errors and the source extraction and subsequent calculation of _ itself. The model for 3C 61.1 used during this investigation is quite basic for such a complex source. This, along with ionospheric effects and the general calibration accuracy of the instrument at the time, can have quite a substantial effect on such a bright source, with such calibration errors not included in this analysis.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C61.1 as a radio-galaxy calibration source, giving its 151.5 MHz flux and modeling its complex morphology with delta functions, shapelets, and a log-polynomial spectrum.

quotes.quote: The initial calibration model consists of six sources, viz. 3C61.1 (intrinsic flux F_ int 38 Jy at 151.5 MHz; baldwin1985 ), 3C220.3 ( F_ int 38 Jy at 150 MHz; cohen2007 ), LQAC 244+085 001 ( F_ int 6.2 Jy at 151.5 MHz; baldwin1985 ), NVSS J011045+873822 ( F_ int 5.1 Jy at 151.5 MHz; baldwin1985 ), NVSS J190350+853648 ( F_ int 4.9 Jy at 151.5 MHz; baldwin1985 ) and NVSS J062205+871948 ( F_ int 4.9 Jy at 151.5 MHz; baldwin1985 ). All sources, except for 3C61.1, are represented by delta functions, and a power-law representing their corresponding source spectra with an assumed, but typical, spectral index of 0.8 . The radio galaxy 3C61.1 has a more complicated morphology, and its model is adapted from the intrinsic sky-model used in the LOFAR-EoR calibration pipeline mertens2020 . The 3C61.1 model uses delta functions and shapelets to represent the source, and a third-order log-polynomial represents the spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper highlights 3C 61.1 as an unusual radio source in a small faint host galaxy within a group, with complex optical tails resembling spiral arms.

quotes.quote: 3C 61.1 at z=0.186 has an unusual radio morphology, and appears to be hosted by an unusually faint, small galaxy at the center of a small group. The optical image shows complex structure dekoff+96 , with tails of emission that resemble spiral arms, and which do not correlate with the radio emission features leahyperley91 . However, the published 20 cm VLA radio map is of low resolution, and it is difficult to confirm the location of the optical-IR counterpart accurately.

sources.summary: The paper argues the detected HRI point source is offset from 3C 61.1 and likely unrelated, so it reports only an upper limit at the radio position.

quotes.quote: 3C 61.1 A faint point source is detected close to the pointing centre of the HRI observation, but it is 30 arcsec from the core of the FRII radio galaxy (Laing Riley, in prep.), a larger offset than would be expected if the sources were associated (see Fig. 61.1fig ). The X-ray source appears to be almost coincident with a point-like optical object in the field, about 30 arcsec to the east of 3C 61.1's host galaxy (see the plates of Gunn \ 1981), which is presumably a background active nucleus. We therefore derive an upper limit on the X-ray counts associated with the radio position.

gmw_00003853.name: 3C 206
gmw_00003853.overview: The central region of the galaxy 3C 206 is saturated, making its host galaxy fit somewhat ambiguous [arXiv:1710.02194]. It can be modeled either as a single bulge or as a disk with a tiny bulge, and it is accompanied by a tiny companion galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper treats the saturated host fit as ambiguous, allowing either a single bulge or bulge-plus-disk model and noting companion contamination in luminosity comparisons.

quotes.quote: 3C 206 (Fig. 11.3) --- \ \ The central region is saturated and there is a tiny companion galaxy. Thus the fit could be a bit ambiguous. The host is fit either with a single bulge ( n = 1.6 ; 18.12 mag) or with a tiny bulge ( R_e = 0.9 pix; 17.95 mag) and a disk ( B/T = 0.55 ).

Interestingly, our estimation of the bulge luminosity is an order of magnitude smaller (fainter) than that ( 16.9 mag) from Hamilton et al. (2008). While the reason for the substantial difference is unclear, Hamilton et al. reported that their photometry method using the PSF-subtracted image systematically overestimates the host luminosity. In this particular object, companion galaxies might contribute to the discrepancy.

gmw_00003854.name: 3C 287.1
gmw_00003854.overview: 3C 287.1 is an active FR II/BLO galaxy with a host that is well-fit by a single bulge component [arXiv:1710.02194, arXiv:2010.11195]. Although several companion objects are located nearby, the galaxy shows no clear signs of interaction [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper fits the host with a single bulge, notes companions but no clear interaction, and argues previous disk-like classification may be nucleus-driven.

quotes.quote: 3C 287.1 (Fig. 11.4) --- \ \ The host is fit with a single bulge. There are several companion objects. But we find no clear sign of interaction.

While Donzelli et al. (2007) claimed that this object has disk-like structure, Dunlop et al. (2003) fit the host with a single component. It is possible that Donzelli et al. misclassified the host due to the bright nucleus. The bulge luminosity from Dunlop et al. (2003) is in good agreement with that from our method.

sources.summary: 3C 287.1 is an FR II/BLO with a marginally resolved central ELR and a detached southern diffuse ELR with large velocity offset and complex excitation.

quotes.quote: 3C 287.1: FR II/BLO, z=0.216, 1 = 3.53 kpc (see Fig. 3c287.1 ). The central ELR is only marginally resolved. At a distance of 5 \ toward the S, in a direction almost perpendicular to the radio jet, a detached diffuse ELR is also seen, with a large velocity offset of 400 \ with respect to the nuclear component. The / \ ratio has a complex structure. In particular the southern component of the ELR shows a strong gradient in / .

gmw_00003855.name: ESO 031 G8
gmw_00003855.overview: ESO 031 G8 is classified as a barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194]. Structural modeling of the galaxy reveals a host composed of a bulge, a bar, and a disk with spiral arms [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper models the saturated host with a bulge, bar, and spiral-arm disk, consistent with earlier barred-spiral classifications.

quotes.quote: ESO 031 G8 (Fig. 11.6) --- \ \ The image is saturated in the center. The fit is done with a bulge, a bar, and a disk with spiral arms. We use the hybrid method for the disk. The host is classified as a barred spiral galaxy from previous studies (Crenshaw et al. 2003; Malkan et al. 1998).

gmw_00003856.name: Mrk 42
gmw_00003856.overview: Mrk 42 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1512.02436] hosted by a galaxy with a pseudo-bulge, a bar, a spiral disk, and an inner ring [arXiv:1710.02194]. The system features a starburst ring at 300 parsecs and far-infrared star formation across the whole galaxy [arXiv:2007.11327].

sources.summary: The paper includes Mrk 42 in a three-object NLS1 application, interpreting redward Fe II and H shifts as gravitational redshifts and deriving unusually large virial factors.

quotes.quote: Mrk 493, IRAS 04416+1215 and Mrk 42 have the redward shifted optical Fe ii and H lines with respect to [ O iii ] 5007 . The virialized motions of BLR clouds are assumed to estimate M_ RM in the RM of H for these NLS1s Du14,Wa14 . The redward shifts of H and Fe ii may be the gravitational redshifts. The Fe ii lag is consistent with the H lag for the same source (see Table 3). Mrk 493 and Mrk 42 have the Fe ii v_ FWHM indistinguishable from the H v_ FWHM .

There are no obvious stratification for these two emission lines in the BLRs for Mrk 493, IRAS 04416 and Mrk 42. Here, we regard these redward shifts of the Fe ii and H lines as the gravitational redshifts. The details for these three NLS1s are listed in Table 3.

sources.summary: The paper describes a saturated WFPC2 host with pseudo-bulge, bar, spiral disk, and inner ring, matching earlier multi-component decompositions.

quotes.quote: Mrk 42 (Fig. 11.14) --- \ \ The WFPC2 image is saturated in the center. The host galaxy appears to possess a pseudo-bulge, a bar, and a disk with spiral arms, modeled with the hybrid method. An inner ring is apparent in the residual image. Jiang et al. (2011) and Orban de Xivry et al. (2011) also fit the host with a bulge, a bar, and a disk components. The bulge to total ratio ( B/T sim 0.12-0.18 ) found by Jiang et al. and Orban de Xivry et al. is in good agreement with our estimation ( B/T=0.16 ).

sources.summary: The paper includes Mrk 42 as the object without reliable Fe II and H beta lags, attributing the failure to a scattered, structureless continuum light curve.

quotes.quote: The left columns of Figure fig-lc and Figure fig-lc42 show the light curves of , , and \ for nine objects with reliable lag measurements, and the remaining one (Mrk 42), respectively. For the variability amplitude ratio of \ to , the range is from about 0.6 (Mrk 486) to 1.2 (Mrk 42), except for Mrk 382 which has the largest host galaxy contamination. All objects in our sample, except for Mrk 42, have reliable time lag measurements for both \ and . The failure to obtain reliable \ and \ in Mrk 42 is caused by the \ light curve, which has large scatter and no clear structure (see Figure fig-lc42 ).

sources.summary: The paper says Mrk 42 has whole-galaxy far-infrared star formation, but its 300 pc starburst ring and VLA scale fall short of explaining the observed radio emission.

quotes.quote: Five objects in our sample have the lowest requirement of _ SFR <10 M_ yr^ 1 kpc^ 2 in producing the observed radio emission, which are Mrk 42, Mrk 684, PG 1115+407, SDSS J010712.04+140845.0 and SDSS J114008.71+030711.4. Mrk 42 and Mrk 684 have a far-infrared luminosity L_ FIR 10^ 10 L_ , suggesting a high SFRs on the scale of the whole galaxy, that does not result in a high star formation rate surface density. Particularly, Mrk 42 was found to have a starburst ring at 300 pc 2007AJ....134..648M and have a total ring SFR of 1.38 M_ yr^ 1 2018MNRAS.477.1086H , which is still below the lower limit required to produce the observed radio emission. Moreover, the VLA A-array has a resolution of 200 pc at C-band for Mrk 42, which suggests that the radio emission from Mrk 42 is less likely to result from the star-forming activity.

gmw_00003857.name: Mrk 352
gmw_00003857.overview: Mrk 352 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194]. Although some visual inspections classified it as an elliptical galaxy, structural fitting reveals the host is a disk galaxy composed of both bulge and disk components [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper fits the saturated host with bulge and disk components, matching one B/T measurement while contrasting with visual elliptical classifications.

quotes.quote: Mrk 352 (Fig. 11.15) --- \ \ The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated. The host galaxy is fit with a bulge and a disk components. The B/T=0.41 found in this study matches well with that ( B/T=0.44 ) derived in Orban de Xivry et al. (2011). However, previous studies (Malkan et al. 1998 and Crenshaw et al. 2003) based on visual inspection conclude that the host is an elliptical galaxy.

gmw_00003858.name: Mrk 423
gmw_00003858.overview: Mrk 423 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy that appears to be interacting [arXiv:1710.02194]. The host galaxy features a pseudo-bulge component, a disk with spiral arms, and tidal tails [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper describes the WFPC2 host as associated with an interacting galaxy and fits it with pseudo-bulge, spiral disk, and tidal tails.

quotes.quote: Mrk 423 (Fig. 11.16) --- \ \ The host galaxy in the WFPC2 mosaic image appears to be associated with a interacting galaxy. The host is fit with a pseudo-bulge component, a disk with spiral arms, and tidal tails. As with Mrk 382, we slightly underestimate B/T ( 0.24 ) compared to Orban de Xivry et al. (2011; B/T 0.42 ).

gmw_00003859.name: Mrk 516
gmw_00003859.overview: Mrk 516 is a merging system that features a host galaxy with a bulge component and a spiral disk [arXiv:1710.02194]. The galaxy contains double nuclei separated by 0.2 arcseconds, and deeper imaging suggests the presence of shell-like structures likely formed during a minor merger [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper fits a bulge and spiral disk, notes double nuclei suggesting a merger, and cites possible shell-like structures from deeper imaging.

quotes.quote: Mrk 516 (Fig. 11.17) --- \ \ The host galaxy is fit with a bulge component and a disk with spiral arms in this WFPC2 mosaic image. From the double nuclei (separation of 0 2 ) it appears that the host is merging system (see also Deo et al. 2006), however there are no other clear signs of interaction in the WFPC2 image. Smirnova et al. (2010) claim that a wide-field deep image shows shell-like structures, which might be formed during minor mergers.

gmw_00003860.name: Mrk 543
gmw_00003860.overview: The host galaxy Mrk 543 features a disk with spiral arms and a pseudo-bulge-like component [arXiv:1710.02194]. Although no clear signs of interaction are visible in space-based imaging, deeper ground-based observations reveal the presence of shells [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper finds a pseudo-bulge-like component and spiral disk, with no HST interaction signature but shells visible in deeper ground-based imaging.

quotes.quote: Mrk 543 (Fig. 11.17) --- \ \ The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated at the center. The host galaxy has a pseudo-bulge-like ( n=1.51 ) component and a disk with spiral arms. Although no clear signs of interaction are present in the \ image, shells are seen in a deep ground-based image from Smirnova et al. (2010).

gmw_00003861.name: Mrk 699
gmw_00003861.overview: Mrk 699 is an S0 host galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194] that contains an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1005.0373]. The active nucleus features a compact narrow-line region [arXiv:2010.12595] and a viewing angle that allows a direct view of its broad-line region [arXiv:2306.12500].

sources.summary: The paper presents NIR spectroscopy for III Zw 77, confirms a high-ionization 7611 line in it, and infers a compact narrow-line region for the closest CLiF AGN.

quotes.quote: IRTF/SpeX data NIR spectra of III Zw 77 and Mrk 1388 were obtained at the NASA 3 m Infrared Telescope Facility (IRTF) using the SpeX spectrograph rayner/2003 in the short cross-dispersed mode (SXD, 0.7-2.4 m). The detector consists of a Teledyne 2048 2048 Hawaii-2RG array with a spatial scale of 0.10 arcsec/ pixel.

had already noted the presence of an unidentified feature at 7613.1 2.8 in III Zw 77 but he did not associated it with any particular emission-line. were the first to attribute the line at 7611 detected in NGC 1068 to . Here, we confirm the identification of \ in III Zw 77 and MRK 1388.
For the closest objects (ESO 138-G 001, NGC 424, Mrk 1388 and III Zw 77), it translates to scales down to 400 pc. Therefore, the NLR of these objects should be very compact.

sources.summary: The paper detects IIIZw77 as a faint X-ray source with a hard excess, interpreting the hard component as an obscured active nucleus with high absorbing column.

quotes.quote: IIIZw77 An \ observation with a net exposure time of 65 ks has detected a faint \ source at this galaxy. The \ spectrum (see Fig. A.2) shows a hard \ excess above 3 keV when a simple power-law is fitted. This hard \ component probably originates from an obscured active nucleus. When modeled by an absorbed power-law with photon index = 1.8 , the absorbing column density is estimated to be N_ H =8^ +5 _ 4 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 . The data have no sufficient quality to constrain a Fe K emission-line.

sources.summary: The paper masks cosmic-ray artifacts, fits a saturated host with bulge and disk, and concludes the host can be classified as S0.

quotes.quote: Mrk 699 (Fig. 11.18) --- \ \ Since cosmic rays are not removed perfectly we mask out pixels affected by cosmic rays. The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated in the center. The host galaxy is fit with a bulge ( n=2.26 ), and a disk component. Alternatively, a fit with a classical bulge ( n=4 ) and a disk finds a bulge component that is brighter by 0.4 mag. We conclude that the host can be classified as an S0 galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper finds III Zw 77 has a low-frequency inclination value, unusual torus radius ratio, optical depth near comparison peaks, and direct central-source viewing with BLR evidence.

quotes.quote: The main results of the comparison between each parameter using the gonzalez/2019 as a reference, can be summarized as follows: The viewing angle, i : The five objects best fitted in this work with skirtor display torus inclination angle i between 10 and 50 deg. In the gonzalez/2019 distribution the least frequent values of i are 20 and 30 deg, which are the values obtained for SDSS J164+43 and III ZW 77, respectively. Moreover, the AGN that is best-fit with clumpy (ESO 138 G1), has an i value of 0 deg. It is located in the second largest peak of the distribution in gonzalez/2019 . The number of dusty clouds in the direction of the line of sight, N_ 0 : In the only AGN that was fit with clumpy (ESO 138 G1) the value of N_ 0 =14 is close to the highest peak of the gonzalez/2019 distribution. The ratio between the outer and inner radius of the torus, ( Y ): The values of 10 and 30 obtained here with skirtor coincide with the two peaks of the distribution in gonzalez/2019 . In SDSS J164+43 and III ZW 77, the Y of 10 and 30, respectively, is not present in their distribution.

The edge-on optical depth at 9.7 microns, _ 9.7 m : The values of 5, 5 and 7 found for III ZW 77, MRK 1388 and 2MASX J113+16, respectively, coincide with the peaks of the distribution in gonzalez/2019 .
Likewise, in III Zw 77 the viewing angle allows a direct view to the central source, in agreement with the detection of a truly BLR.

gmw_00003862.name: Mrk 871
gmw_00003862.overview: Mrk 871 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy with a spiral disk [arXiv:1710.02194]. The galaxy's structure includes a bar and a pseudo-bulge [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper finds the saturated host has a pseudo-bulge, bar, and spiral disk, with B/T and bulge index agreeing with prior measurements.

quotes.quote: Mrk 871 (Fig. 11.19) --- \ \ The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated in the center. The best fit finds that the host galaxy has a pseudo-bulge ( n=1.8 ), a bar and a disk with spiral arms. Orban de Xivry et al. (2011) found B/T 0.11 and n_ bul 1.3 , which is in good agreement with our measurements ( B/T 0.13 ).

gmw_00003863.name: Mrk 896
gmw_00003863.overview: Mrk 896 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194] classified as a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0410374]. Its structure features a pseudo-bulge, a bar, and inner and outer spiral disks [arXiv:1710.02194].

sources.summary: The paper uses an archival Mrk 896 spectrum to measure stellar kinematics, notes Ca II emission overlap, and treats its uncertainty differently because only one template was available.

quotes.quote: Mrk 896 was extracted from the Isaac Newton Group (ING) Archive. A spectrum covering a larger range ( 7700--9400 ) was obtained in August 1994 at the William Herschel Telescope (Canary Islands, Spain) with ISIS Double Beam Spectrograph. The R316R grating used in combination with a 1.47 arcsec-slit yielded a dispersion of 1.35 /px and an instrumental resolution of 65 . A standard star (BD+17 4708) was observed during the same run for spectrophotometric calibration.

In particular, 5 out of the 8 observed targets show the O i 8446 emission line, and in at least 3 galaxies (Mrk 335, Mrk 896, UGC 3478) the Ca ii absorptions are overlapped to broader Ca ii emissions. Then, FXCOR was run using two template stars for the Asiago data, and the only one available for the ING archival data.
_1 was not estimated for Mrk 896, since only one template star was available.

sources.summary: The paper fits the saturated AGN host with a pseudo-bulge, bar, and inner and outer spiral disks, comparing the result to prior bulge-disk modeling.

quotes.quote: Mrk 896 (Fig. 11.19) --- \ \ The WFPC2 mosaic image is saturated in the center. The AGN host is fit with a pseudo-bulge ( n=1.43 ), a bar, two (outer and inner) disks with spiral arms ( B/T 0.11 ), components . Orbad de Xivry et al. (2011) fit the host with a bulge ( n=2.06 ) and a disk ( B/T 0.15 ).

gmw_00003864.name: Mrk 1126
gmw_00003864.overview: Mrk 1126 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:1710.02194] containing a pseudo-bulge and two spiral disks [arXiv:1710.02194]. The galaxy exhibits non-rotational or non-planar gas motions [arXiv:1401.7666] and shows species-dependent velocity-dispersion differences across its central kiloparsec [arXiv:1401.7666].

sources.summary: The paper reports H2 behavior unlike most galaxies and a centrally peaked [ FeII ] profile in NGC 7450, non-rotational or non-planar gas motions, and marked species-dependent velocity-dispersion differences across its central kiloparsec.

quotes.quote: The H _2 line-to-continuum ratio is constant or drops significantly towards the nucleus in most galaxies, except NGC 2273, NGC 5273, and NGC 7450. For Br , in contrast, the ratio peaks towards the nucleus in many galaxies, consistent with stronger ionization mechanisms towards the active nucleus. These two results both explain the observed central drop of the H _2 /Br ratio. Of the three emission lines, the [ FeII ] line-to-continuum ratio shows the most distinct radial variations. Its profile is centrally peaked in Mrk 1, Mrk 3, NGC 262, NGC 1320, NGC 2273, NGC 3081, NGC 5273, and NGC 7450. Several sources show two maxima (or a central drop, e.g. NGC 1667, NGC 3185, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, NGC 4388, and NGC 5506). Finally, Mrk 1066, NGC 931, NGC 2110, NGC 2992, and NGC 4051 show peculiar distributions, e.g. the distribution in NGC 2110 shows three separate peaks.

However, the gas velocity curves are similar to those of the stars, which define the galaxy disk. This is the case in Mrk 1, Mrk 1066, NGC 262, NGC 931, NGC 1667, NGC 2110, NGC 2273, NGC 3081, NGC 3185, NGC 4388, NGC 5273, and NGC 5506. In these galaxies, all three emission lines display a velocity curve that follows that of the stars and is reminiscent of rotation, thus strongly implying that the line-emitting gas is located in the disk of the galaxy and rotating with the disk. Other galaxies (Mrk 3, NGC 1320, NGC 2992, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, and NGC 7450) display velocity curves different from the stellar velocity curve in one or more emission lines, which may be a signature of non-rotational motion, either in- or outflows or other non-planar motion of the gas.

The radial dispersion profiles in Mrk 3, Mrk 1066, NGC 2110, NGC 2273, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, NGC 5273, and NGC 5506 have a central peak, which indicates a higher kinetic gas temperature in the nucleus. Either the ISM is more turbulent (possibly caused by a jet- or wind-driven outflow), and/or more intensely heated by the incident radiation field. In either case, a peaked dispersion distribution is a strong indication for the AGN governing the physical state of the gas at the nucleus. The dispersion peak is most often present in [ FeII ] (indicating dust destruction close to the nucleus), and/or in Br (indicating a strong radiation field), while the H _2 line shows the least-pronounced central rise. Examples for flat velocity dispersion profiles are NGC 3081, NGC 3185, and NGC 4388, which show no significant change over up to 1.2 kpc in diameter. The velocity dispersion in all three sources is of order 100 km/s, well within the range observed for star forming regions, which is consistent with star formation being the dominant the gas excitation mechanism. Finally, the dispersion profiles in Mrk 1, NGC 262, NGC 931, NGC 1320, NGC 1667, NGC 2992, and NGC 7450 show marked differences between the three emission lines, with variations of 100 km/s or more at a given radius. This indicates that the ionized atomic and molecular gas is responding differently to the central AGN in these sources, perhaps due to the presences of asymmetric energy input (a jet or shocks) together with a stratified medium, e.g. a thin molecular disk in a thick ionized medium.

sources.summary: The paper uses a WFPC2 mosaic to fit the AGN host with pseudo-bulge and two spiral disks, including an inner lens-like disk component.

quotes.quote: Mrk 1126 (Fig. 11.20) --- \ \ The image is slightly saturated. We use a mosaic image of WFPC2 for the fit. The AGN host galaxy is fit with a pseudo-bulge and two disks with spiral arms. The inner disk component has n=0.5 , which suggests that it can be a ``lens" (Kormendy 1979). The fitting result is consistent with Orbad de Xivry et al.

gmw_00003865.name: WISE J082501.48+300257.2
gmw_00003865.overview: WISE J082501.48+300257.2 is a dust-obscured galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.89 [arXiv:1911.04702]. It hosts a Compton-thick active galactic nucleus powered by an actively growing supermassive black hole [arXiv:1911.04702].

sources.summary: The paper reports WISE J082501.48+300257.2 as a z=0.89 Compton-thick dust-obscured AGN with high X-ray luminosity, large column density, and an actively growing black hole.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of a Compton-thick (CT) dust-obscured galaxy (DOG) at z = 0.89, WISE J082501.48+300257.2 (WISE0825+3002), observed by Nuclear Spectroscopic Telescope Array ( NuSTAR ). X-ray analysis with the XCLUMPY model revealed that hard X-ray luminosity in the rest-frame 2--10 keV band of WISE0825+3002 is L_ X (2--10 keV) = 4.2^ +2.8 _ 1.6 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 while its hydrogen column density is N_ H = 1.0^ +0.8 _ 0.4 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 , indicating that WISE0825+3002 is a mildly CT active galactic nucleus (AGN). We performed the spectral energy distribution (SED) fitting with CIGALE to derive its stellar mass, star formation rate, and infrared luminosity. The estimated Eddington ratio based on stellar mass and integration of the best-fit SED of AGN component is _ Edd = 0.70, which suggests that WISE0825+3002 harbors an actively growing black hole behind a large amount of gas and dust.

gmw_00003866.name: NGC 5954
gmw_00003866.overview: NGC 5954 is a late-type, interacting companion galaxy that forms a binary system with NGC 5953 [arXiv:0911.3772]. Classified as a LINER or Seyfert 2 galaxy, it exhibits a distorted morphology, a tidal bridge, and circumnuclear starburst activity [arXiv:0911.3772].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5954 as the late-type interacting companion to NGC 5953, sharing a binary system, tidal structures, circumnuclear starburst activity, and joint stellar decomposition modeling.

quotes.quote: \ and its late-type companion NGC 5954 (LINER/Seyfert 2, SAB(rs)cd pec) constitute a binary system (VV 244, Arp 91) where the two galaxies are separated by a projected distance of 5.8 kpc ( 43 ). They show clear signs of interaction visible in the distorted morphology, the presence of a tidal bridge (or distorted arm) connecting the two galaxies, and of prominent star-forming regions. Both galaxies have circumnuclear starbursts that may have been induced by the interaction gonzalez96 . The NGC 5953/54 pair has also been mapped in atomic and molecular gas.

To investigate stellar structure, we performed a two-dimensional B/D decomposition with galfit peng02 on both the ground-based H band image and IRAC 3.6 m image, fitting both galaxies (NGC 5953 and NGC 5954) simultaneously. The best fit included a Sersic generalized exponential bulge, an exponential disk, and a nuclear point source for , and a Sersic generalized exponential bulge (but with index n 1, so this is probably in reality a disk) and a nuclear point source for NGC 5954. The IRAC fit has a reduced ^2_ of 4.2 (averaged over \ and NGC 5954), roughly ten times smaller than that in the H band.

gmw_00003867.name: ESO079-007
gmw_00003867.overview: ESO079-007 is classified as a peculiar S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1808.04491]. It features asymmetric star-forming regions that are concentrated more toward one side of the galactic center than the other [arXiv:1808.04491].

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO079-007 as a peculiar S0 with asymmetric star-forming regions concentrated on one side of its center, visible in emission-line spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: ESO079-007 The NASA Extragalactic Database (NED), describes this S0 galaxy as being peculiar. What sets this galaxy apart from conventional S0 galaxies is the presence of asymmetric star forming regions i.e. the star forming regions are more towards one side of the centre than the other. To illustrate this better, we show in Figure fig:eso079_asy , the 2-d spectrum near the wavelength region dominated by H , N [ II ] and S [ II ] emission lines. The figure shows the stellar continuum in the centre of the galaxy. It is easy to see that there is a stronger star formation activity on one side of the galaxy centre than the the opposite side.

gmw_00003868.name: NGC 1522
gmw_00003868.overview: NGC 1522 is classified as a peculiar S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1808.04491]. Its optical light is dominated by a very young, highly metal-poor star-forming population, despite having a faded disk component [arXiv:1808.04491].

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 1522 as a peculiar S0 whose optical light is dominated by a very young, metal-poor star-forming population despite a faded disk component.

quotes.quote: NGC 1522 NED classifies this galaxy as a peculiar S0 galaxy. The spectrum of this galaxy suggests a very young population and strong ongoing star formation dominating the light budget at optical wavelengths. starlight models suggest that 95 of the flux is explained by a stellar population younger than 10^8 yr.

What makes the object even more interesting is that it is highly metal poor suggesting that the gas responsible for the ongoing star formation is not enriched by any Population 2 stars. Bulge-disk decomposition for this galaxy shows that the bulge component of the stars accounts for 40 of the total galaxy light and the rest by a faded disk. Yet again we have a case of a galaxy whose star formation indicates availability of a large amount of gas but the disk shows signs of having faded, assuming a spiral progenitor with a brighter disk.

gmw_00003869.name: IC 2085
gmw_00003869.overview: IC 2085 is a Dorado Group galaxy that contains both very old and very young stellar populations in its center [arXiv:1808.04491]. The galaxy exhibits ongoing star formation, an asymmetric central age gradient, and disturbed dust lanes [arXiv:1808.04491].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 2085 as a Dorado Group galaxy with mixed old and young central stellar populations, ongoing star formation, asymmetric age gradients, and disturbed dust lanes.

quotes.quote: IC 2085 This galaxy is part of NGC 1566 group of galaxies which is a part of the Dorado Group. In comparison to other galaxies in this sample, the spectrum obtained for this galaxy is of a relatively lower SNR. Even without performing any modelling, one can see that this galaxy contains both a very old and a very young stellar population in the centre. The presence of strong emission lines such as H support ongoing star formation as well as recently formed stars while the 4000 \ break caused by several absorption features below 4000 \ is indicative of an older population. Figure 9 shows an asymmetric age gradient around the centre however the stellar metallicity distribution is symmetric.

This could be due to the bursts of star formation in the presence of asymmetric dust features. The optical image from Digital Sky Survey (DSS) confirms the presence of disturbed dust lanes. The best-fit model from starlight reveals that 50 of the light is accounted for by a young population with age <10^8 yr and the rest by very old stars.

gmw_00003870.name: ESO085-030
gmw_00003870.overview: ESO085-030 is a galaxy characterized by a central bulge-dominated pseudobulge system [arXiv:1808.04491]. It features a high bulge-to-total ratio of 0.6, with old stars accounting for approximately 40 percent of the light in its center [arXiv:1808.04491].

sources.summary: The paper says ESO085-030 resembles ESO079-007 without asymmetric star formation and has an unusually high B/T ratio, implying a central bulge-dominated pseudobulge system.

quotes.quote: ESO085-030 This galaxy shares several features with ESO079-007 except for the star forming regions which are not distributed asymmetrically. About 40 of the light in the centre of this galaxy is being accounted for by old stars and this fraction falls to 15 as one moves outwards. The B/T ratio as determined from the 3.6-micron imaging is 0.6 with the disk scale length smaller than the bulge effective radius and the Sersic index is highest in the sample. This means that at least in the centre, the bulge which is surrounded by the small disk dominates significantly and thus the trend being observed is due to the bulge. This indicates that pseudobulges grow in a manner similar to disks i.e. inside-out.

gmw_00003871.name: WISE J102905.90+050132.4
gmw_00003871.overview: WISE J102905.90+050132.4 is an infrared-bright dust-obscured galaxy and an ultraluminous infrared galaxy at a redshift of 0.493 [arXiv:1711.10091]. Although its optical spectrum reveals an active galactic nucleus outflow, the galaxy exhibits normal molecular gas properties with no signs of a strong molecular gas outflow [arXiv:1711.10091].

sources.summary: The paper presents WISE J102905.90+050132.4 as an infrared-bright DOG/ULIRG with strong ionized outflow but normal CO molecular gas and no strong molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: For one of these DOGs (WISE J102905.90+050132.4) at z_ spec = 0.493 , we performed follow-up observations using ALMA to investigate their CO molecular gas properties. As a result, we successfully detected ^ 12 CO( J =2--1) and ^ 12 CO( J =4--3) lines, and the continuum of this DOG. The intensity-weighted velocity map of both lines shows a gradient, and the line profile of those CO lines is well-fitted by a single narrow Gaussian, meaning that this DOG has no sign of strong molecular gas outflow. The IR luminosity of this object is (L_ IR /L_ ) = 12.40 that is classified as ultraluminous IR galaxy (ULIRG). We found that (i) the stellar mass and star-formation rate relation and (ii) the CO luminosity and far-IR luminosity relation are consistent with those of typical ULIRGs at similar redshifts.

These results indicate that the molecular gas properties of this DOG are normal despite that its optical spectrum showing a powerful AGN outflow. We conclude that a powerful ionized-gas outflow caused by the AGN does not necessarily affect the cold interstellar medium in the host galaxy at least for this DOG.

gmw_00003872.name: NGC 5490
gmw_00003872.overview: NGC 5490 is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2109.14644] and the brightest galaxy in the Zwicky cluster 1407.6+1750 [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256]. It features a compact central dust structure, a central emission peak, and hosts a double-sided radio jet [arXiv:astro-ph/9909256].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5490 as the elliptical host of two fast-declining Type Ia supernova siblings and derives consistent host distance moduli from several methods.

quotes.quote: SN 2015bo has a SN twin (similar spectra) and sibling (same host galaxy), SN 1997cn. Distance moduli of = 34.33 0.01 (stat) 0.11 (sys) mag and = 34.34 0.04 (stat) 0.12 (sys) mag are derived for SN 2015bo and SN 1997cn, respectively. These distances are consistent at the 0.06 level with each other, and they are also consistent with distances derived using surface-brightness fluctuations and redshift-corrected cosmology.

Since fast decliners are located preferentially in early-type galaxies Hamuy96 , it is unsurprising that an early-type galaxy is the host of two fast-declining SNe as opposed to a different galaxy type where fast-declining SNe Ia are even less common . This provides an opportunity to evaluate the accuracy and consistency of these quasi-rare sub-types as distance indicators. To date, this is the only known instance of two fast-declining SNe exploding in the same host galaxy. In this section, distance moduli are derived using various methods and checked for consistency. We calculate the distance modulus to the host (NGC 5490) with three independent methods: i) the z_ cmb distance derived method assuming a cosmology of H_ 0 =73 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , _ m =0.27 and _ =0.73, ii) the independent distance derived using the photometry of all available bands of , and iii) the independent distance derived using the photometry of all available bands of .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5490 as the brightest galaxy in a Zwicky cluster, with a compact central dust structure, emission peak, and double-sided radio jet.

quotes.quote: NGC 5490 NGC 5490 is the brightest galaxy in Zwicky cluster 1407.6+1750. There is a very small central dust distribution 0.5'' (170 pc) in size with PA 143 . It appears to be either a dust lane or a highly inclined disk. There is a compact central peak of \ emission. NGC 5490 hosts a double-sided radio jet (CB88; Xu 1999a).

Our isophotal analysis agrees with a study by De Juan (1994). They found a lower ellipticity in the inner few arcseconds, but poor seeing is the most likely cause for this. They also note a 15 increase of PA outward of 10'' .

gmw_00003873.name: Fnx
aliases_external: Fornax dwarf spheroidal selection center in paper
gmw_00003873.overview: Fnx is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy with a stellar population spanning a wide range of metallicities [arXiv:2109.05130]. Chemical abundance analyses of its stars suggest a high relative contribution from Type Ia supernovae and asymptotic giant branch stars, as well as support for a second epoch of star formation [arXiv:2109.05130].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Fnx APOGEE abundances, emphasizing its anomalous abundance ratios, high Type Ia and AGB contributions, and evidence for a secondary star-formation epoch.

quotes.quote: The APOGEE data, shown in the right columns of Figure fig:mw_comp and fig:mw_comp_mg , contains Fnx stars with -2.2 < [ Fe/H ] < 0.5. However, because the Fnx sample is generally lower in S/N as compared to the other galaxies, most of our analysis is focused on the Fnx stars with [ Fe/H ] > -1.2 , as the stars more metal poor than this have larger abundance uncertainties.

Fnx is the most striking outlier among the five dwarf galaxies, in [ O/Fe ] , [ Mg/Fe ] , [ Si/Fe ] , [ Ni/Fe ] , [ (C+N)/Mg ] , [ Ca/Mg ] , and to a lesser extend, [ Ce/Mg ] . Possibly these differences could imply different IMF-averaged yields from Fnx stars, e.g., Type II SNe that produced more Fe relative to elements. Another possibility is winds that preferentially ejected Type II SNe products. Or, Fnx could simply have a SFH that led to an exceptionally high ratio of Type Ia/Type II SNe enrichment. A high relative contribution of Type Ia SNe could explain most of these anomalies, and a similarly high contribution of AGB enrichment could explain high [ (C+N)/Mg ] and [ Ce/Mg ] ratios.

As was the case for Sgr, both the flexCE and Lian models find support for a second epoch of star formation in Fnx, although this second ``burst'' was much weaker than the initial SF peak ( 3 times weaker for the flexCE results and 9 times weaker for the Lian model results).

gmw_00003874.name: NGC 3997
gmw_00003874.overview: NGC 3997 is a barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0607078] that belongs to a small galaxy cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0607078]. It is a merger system of two spiral galaxies that exhibits tidally deformed spiral arms and a tidal tail [arXiv:astro-ph/0607078].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3997 as the barred spiral, multiple merger system hosting SN 2004aw in a tidal tail, with tidally deformed arms, redshift-based distance estimates, and host-galaxy reddening.

quotes.quote: The SN is located at = 11 ^ h 57 ^ m 50 25 and = +25 15 55 1, in a tidal tail of NGC 3997, a barred spiral (SBb) galaxy and multiple system (LEDA Lyon-Meudon Extragalactic Database, http://leda.univ-lyon1.fr/ ). vandenBergh05 noted that NGC 3997 is a merger system of two spiral galaxies showing tidally deformed spiral arms.

Neither Cepheid distances nor other similarly precise distance measurements are available for NGC 3997, the host galaxy of SN 2004aw. Therefore, the redshift is the only indicator of its distance. LEDA reports a recession velocity of 4769 km s ^ 1 inferred mainly from radio measurements, which increases to 4906 km s ^ 1 when a correction for the Local-Group infall onto the Virgo cluster is applied. The recession velocities of the other members of the small galaxy cluster to which NGC 3997 belongs show only little dispersion (approximately 250 km s ^ 1 around an average Local-Group infall corrected value of 4700 km s ^ 1 ).

gmw_00003875.name: J1034
gmw_00003875.overview: The galaxy J1034 is classified as a quasar and is included in the Million Quasar catalog [arXiv:2206.13148]. Spectral energy distribution fitting and optical variability indicate an active galactic nucleus contribution to its emission [arXiv:2206.13148].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1034 as the source of AT2021acak, argues its survey colors, quasar-catalog entry, SED fit, and optical variability indicate an AGN/quasar, complicating the TDE interpretation.

quotes.quote: AT2021acak arises from a source with a position of R.A.= 10^h34^m47^s.99 , Decl.= +15^ 29'22''.42 (J2000.0). Hereafter we use name J1034 for this source.

The source J1034 has been detected in different surveys, which include the Galaxy Evolution Explorer (GALEX; galex11 ), the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS; sdss09 ), the Two Micron All Sky Survey (2MASS; 2mass03 ), and the WISE wise12 . Its infrared colors were J-Ks=1.31 and Ks-W3=4.01 or mid-infrared (MIR) colors were W1-W2=0.78 and W2-W3=2.19 . The both suggest a quasar (e.g., wri+10,tw13 ). Indeed, J1034 is included as a quasar in the Million Quasar catalog (version 7.2; fle21 ).
The result is shown in Figure fig:sed , for which the parameter f_ agn 0.27 (see lej+18 for details). This parameter value suggests a significant AGN contribution in the emission of J1034.

gmw_00003876.name: J1430+4103
gmw_00003876.overview: J1430+4103 is a strong gravitational lensing galaxy located at a redshift of 0.285 [arXiv:1505.00502]. It exhibits a complex morphology with several subcomponents [arXiv:1505.00502], and its dark matter halo has been modeled using fuzzy dark matter parameters [arXiv:2308.14640].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J1430+4105 as a SLACS strong lens with complex substructure, published effective-radius and velocity-dispersion measurements, and detailed prior modeling with SIE, power-law, and two-component models.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1430+4105 SDSS1430+4105 was first described by as part of the SLACS survey. This system is at redshift z_ lens =0.285 with z_ source =0.575, and has a complex morphology with several subcomponents as described by . Bolton reported an effective radius of 2.55" and a _ SDSS =322 km s ^ 1 .

A very detailed lens model analysis of this system was then conducted by . This analysis was a direct, semi-independent comparative analysis using both Gravlens (LTM) and Lensview (non-LTM) software. The authors studied five different models using Gravlens/Lensmodel, including an SIE and a Power Law (PL) model as well as three two-component de Vaucouleurs plus dark matter models.

sources.summary: The paper uses J1430+4103 as an example lensing galaxy whose MCMC fits fail for 10^-24 eV fuzzy dark matter at full fraction but converge at lower fractions.

quotes.quote: Fig. fig:J1430+4105 shows qualitatively the MCMC results for the galaxy J1430+4103 with bosonic mass m_a=10^ 24 eV . As we can see, for this particle mass there in no set of parameters that satisfies Eq. kappa1 for a FDM fraction f_ FDM =1 (the bottom plot). As we lower the fraction, for example to f_ FDM =0.8 (as shown in the upper right plot), we can see that the MCMC starts to converge. For a fraction of f_ FDM =0.1 (in the left upper plot), the MCMC process converged and found a set of parameters that satisfy Eq. kappa1 . The same plots in their full size are given in Appendix app:MCMC plots .

gmw_00003877.name: CFHQS J0227-0605
gmw_00003877.overview: CFHQS J0227-0605 is a high-redshift quasar located at z=6.20 [arXiv:0901.0565]. It exhibits a strong Lyman-alpha emission line and displays an unusually red color that may indicate its continuum is reddened by dust [arXiv:0901.0565].

sources.summary: The paper describes CFHQS J0227-0605 as a z=6.20 CFHTLS Wide quasar with strong Ly-alpha emission, unusually red z'-J color, and possible continuum reddening by dust.

quotes.quote: CFHQS J0227-0605 This quasar is the first to be found from the CFHTLS Wide. With z=6.20 , it is the second highest redshift quasar in the CFHQS so far and the fifth highest redshift quasar known. It has a fairly strong \ emission line. There is an apparent dark region of the IGM at z 6 , but higher S/N spectroscopy is required to determine whether this region does have a high optical depth.

But CFHQS J0227-0605 is 0.5 mag redder than the typical quasar at its redshift. This could be an indication that the quasar continuum is reddened by dust like several other very high redshift quasars (Maiolino et al. 2004; McGreer et al. 2006; Venemans et al. 2007; W07).

gmw_00003878.name: CFHQS J0216-0455
gmw_00003878.overview: CFHQS J0216-0455 is a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus and quasar at a redshift of z=6.01 [arXiv:1110.1722, arXiv:1710.02212]. ALMA observations of the host galaxy show no line or continuum emission, indicating a low star-formation rate [arXiv:1710.02212].

sources.summary: CFHQS J0216 0455 is a very low-luminosity z=6.01 quasar whose ALMA observations detect neither line nor continuum emission, implying a low host-galaxy star-formation limit.

quotes.quote: CFHQS J0216 0455 J0216 0455 is the lowest luminosity quasar discovered in the CFHQS with absolute magnitude M_ 1450 =-22.21 Willott:2009 . The redshift of z=6.01 is based on a strong and narrow \ line, where the broad red wing of this line identifies the object as an AGN rather than a Lyman break galaxy. \ emission has not been detected for this quasar, so the black hole mass of 2 10^7 M_ is instead estimated assuming that it accretes at the Eddington rate Willott:2010 .

In the ALMA data there is no detection of either line or continuum emission. The expected \ redshift is based on the narrow \ line so we do not think it likely that the true position of the \ line is outside the observed band. The adjacent sub-band was searched for a \ line, but no line was seen. The non-detection in both line and continuum is consistent with a low star-formation rate ( 3 limit of 10 M_ yr ^ 1 ) in the host galaxy of this very low black hole mass and luminosity quasar.

sources.summary: The paper describes CFHQS J0216-0455 as a z=6.01 quasar formerly identified as MCD1, with a strong asymmetric emission line and exceptionally low luminosity among z>6 quasars.

quotes.quote: CFHQS J0216-0455 This object was first identified by McLure et al. (2006) as a potential z 6 Lyman Break galaxy based on optical and near-IR photometry (their source name: MCD1). However, the fact that it is unresolved in the z' band ( 0.8'' FWHM) and is relatively bright at z' , suggested that it was also consistent with being a z 6 quasar. The optical spectrum shows a very strong asymmetric emission line and weak continuum on the red side of the line only. It is at a redshift of z=6.01 . The emission line contributes about 70 of the flux detected in the z' band.
With an absolute magnitude of M_ 1450 =-22.21 , this quasar is the least luminous known quasar at z>6 by some margin.

sources.summary: UUDS J021627.80-045534.2 is identified as a z=6.01 low-luminosity AGN/quasar, originally selected as an ultra-luminous LBG candidate and used to argue AGN contamination is negligible.

quotes.quote: One of our targets (UUDS J021627.80-045534.2) is a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus (AGN) at a redshift of z=6.01 (Ly FWHM 1600 km s ^ 1 ). This object was initially identified as a potential ultra-luminous z 6 LBG candidate by McLure et al. (2006) and was subsequently targeted as a high-redshift quasar candidate by Willott et al. (2009). In Willott et al. (2009) this source is referred to as CFHQS J021627 045534, and was confirmed as a low-luminosity quasar at z=6.01 based on a four-hour spectrum obtained with the GMOS spectrograph on Gemini. However, it is important to note that CFHQS J021627 045534 was the only z 6 AGN identified by Willott et al. (2010) over a search area of 4.47 sq. degrees, down to a limit of z^ =24.5 . Based on this fact, and the steepness of the z 6 UV-selected galaxy luminosity function, it seems reasonable to conclude that the luminous, type I AGN contamination of 26 z^ 25 LBG samples at z 6 is negligible.

gmw_00003879.name: CFHQS J0102-0218
gmw_00003879.overview: CFHQS J0102-0218 is a high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of z=5.95 [arXiv:0901.0565]. Its spectrum displays a characteristic Lyman-alpha emission line and a distinct break in the continuum across the line [arXiv:0901.0565].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CFHQS J0102-0218 as a z=5.95 quasar candidate selected from its color limit, with a typical z'-J color, Ly-alpha line, and continuum break.

quotes.quote: CFHQS J0102-0218 This z=5.95 quasar is not detected at i' band due to the relatively poor sensitivity of the i' band observation. The color limit of i'-z'>2.09 enabled it to be identified as a quasar candidate and the z'-J color of z'-J=0.26 is typical of 5.5<z<6.1 quasars. The spectrum shows a typical \ line and a break in the continuum across the line.

gmw_00003880.name: PDF J010837.1 454819
gmw_00003880.overview: PDF J010837.1 454819 is an S0 cluster galaxy that exhibits extended radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its ambiguous emission-line ratios suggest that both active galactic nucleus activity and star formation processes may be present [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J010837.1 454819 as an S0 cluster galaxy with extended radio emission and ambiguous emission-line ratios consistent with both AGN activity and star formation.

quotes.quote: PDF J010837.1 454819: The radio emission in this S0 galaxy is extended. The spectrum shows H , [ N ii ] ( 658 nm) and [ S ii ] ( 672, 673 nm) emission, but no H , [ O iii ] ( 501 nm) or [ O i ] ( 630 nm). The [ N ii ] /H and [ S ii ] /H equivalent width (EW) ratios are 0.5 and 0.4, respectively. In spectral diagnostic diagrams, e.g., VO:87 , such ratios place the galaxy on the locus separating star forming galaxies from Seyfert 2s and LINERs. Both AGN and star formation processes may be present.

gmw_00003881.name: PDF J010904.5 454624
gmw_00003881.overview: PDF J010904.5 454624 is an E/S0 early-type galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413] that is a member of a galaxy cluster [arXiv:2103.06462]. It hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2103.06462] with unresolved radio emission centered on its optical nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper links ESO 243 G 045, an E/S0 low-luminosity AGN cluster member, to the western Jellyfish peak and possible lobe or re-energised tail emission.

quotes.quote: At 215.5 , the radio emission at A and the E/S0 galaxy ESO 243 G 045 are well aligned. There is evidence of a slight southeast elongation of the emission, and indeed, at lower frequencies the western peak shifts southeast slightly from A. Source B is offset slightly to the southwest of S0 galaxy WISEA J010946.55-454657.4. The northwest alignment of the emission of source B at 215.5 remains visible at lower frequencies suggesting continuity of the emission source. Hopkins2000 had previously classified both of these radio sources: ESO 243 G 045 as a low-luminosity AGN and WISEA J010946.55-454657.4 unambiguously as a Seyfert 2 galaxy.

Both peaks are the least steep components of the spectral image map, indicating a cocoon of younger, more energetic electrons that envelop cluster members ESO 243 G 045 and WISEA J010946.55-454657.4. We suggest that the shallower spectrum component at the western peak, though it is offset to the southeast of source A, is directly related to emission from ESO 243 G 045.

The eastern extension from ESO 243 G 045 that is visible at 185.5 is suggestive of either a lobe from a previously active epoch of AGN activity or a tail. If it is the latter, one plausible explanation is that both tentacles are the re-energised tail of ESO 243 G 045 that sweeps south towards the core of the cluster.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PDF J010904.5 454624 with ESO 243 G 045, an E/S0 galaxy whose nuclear radio emission is attributed to an AGN in an evolved stellar population.

quotes.quote: PDF J010904.5 454624: Also catalogued as ESO 243 G 045, this galaxy has an E/S0 morphology and unresolved radio emission centred on the optical nucleus. The spectrum shows no emission and has absorption lines characteristic of an evolved stellar population. The radio emission is likely to be due solely to an AGN.

gmw_00003882.name: PDF J010946.5 454657
gmw_00003882.overview: PDF J010946.5 454657 is an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2103.06462, arXiv:astro-ph/0001413] with radio emission centered on its optical nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its emission-line spectrum unambiguously identifies the system as a Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:2103.06462, arXiv:astro-ph/0001413] that may host historic, weak active galactic nucleus jets [arXiv:2103.06462].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J010946.55-454657.4 as an S0 Seyfert 2 galaxy near source B, whose historic weak AGN jets may form the Jellyfish eastern peak and spur.

quotes.quote: Source B is offset slightly to the southwest of S0 galaxy WISEA J010946.55-454657.4. The northwest alignment of the emission of source B at 215.5 remains visible at lower frequencies suggesting continuity of the emission source.
