Hopkins2000 had previously classified both of these radio sources: ESO 243 G 045 as a low-luminosity AGN and WISEA J010946.55-454657.4 unambiguously as a Seyfert 2 galaxy.

Both peaks are the least steep components of the spectral image map, indicating a cocoon of younger, more energetic electrons that envelop cluster members ESO 243 G 045 and WISEA J010946.55-454657.4. We suggest that the shallower spectrum component at the western peak, though it is offset to the southeast of source A, is directly related to emission from ESO 243 G 045. We also suggest that the elongated eastern peak is the product of a pair of weak AGN jets originating from source B, WISEA J010946.55-454657.4; the slight offset likely indicates that this activity was historic. Moreover, the eastern spur of the jellyfish head can be explained by deflection of the south eastern jet of source B by the denser, more central ICM.

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J010946.5 454657 as an S0 galaxy with nuclear radio emission and emission-line diagnostics favoring a Seyfert 2 interpretation.

quotes.quote: PDF J010946.5 454657: This galaxy has an S0 morphology, with radio contours centred on the optical nucleus. The spectrum shows emission lines which unambiguously identify it as a Seyfert 2 or LINER in spectral diagnostic diagrams. The absence of strong [ O i ] emission and the presence of strong [ O iii ] emission suggest a Seyfert 2 interpretation Heck:80 . The galaxy was also detected by CR:97 with an emission-line spectrum (their Figure 10a, spectrum 43b).

gmw_00003883.name: PDF J010947.9 455125
gmw_00003883.overview: PDF J010947.9 455125 is an S0 lenticular galaxy that hosts a central radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its emission-line ratios suggest the presence of both active galactic nucleus and star-formation processes, potentially within a post-starburst system [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J010947.9 455125 as an S0 radio source with emission-line ratios suggesting mixed AGN and star-formation processes, possibly in a post-starburst system.

quotes.quote: PDF J010947.9 455125: This S0 galaxy has radio contours centred on the optical nucleus. The spectrum shows H , [ N ii ] and [ S ii ] emission, while H is present in absorption, and no [ O iii ] is detected. The [ N ii ] /H and [ S ii ] /H EW ratios of 0.6 and 0.3, respectively, give conflicting suggestions as to the nature of the activity. The relative strength of the [ N ii ] /H emission is suggestive of a Seyfert 2 nucleus, but [ S ii ] and [ O iii ] are rather weak. Both AGN and star-formation processes may be present.

gmw_00003884.name: PDF J010955.5 455552
gmw_00003884.overview: PDF J010955.5 455552, also known as IC 1633, is a cD galaxy [arXiv:2103.06462, arXiv:astro-ph/0001413] located at the center of the Abell 2877 cluster [arXiv:2103.06462]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2103.06462] and exhibits an evolved stellar population [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413] with faint radio emission that may indicate a small jet or lobe [arXiv:2103.06462, arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 1633 as Abell 2877's central cD galaxy, an AGN host with faint possible jet emission and a candidate third electron source for the Jellyfish.

quotes.quote: Source cD is the central cluster galaxy, IC 1633, and in the lower frequency bands the western tentacle bridges this source to the rest of the emission. It is identified by Hopkins2000 as hosting an AGN, and both their ATCA observation and ours show nearby faint emission to the northeast that may indicate a small jet.

Both the western and eastern peaks of emission at the head of the USS Jellyfish are associated with flatter spectra, as is the emission coincident with the cD galaxy itself and the background radio galaxy E.

An alternative explanation would be to invoke the cD galaxy IC 1633 as a third electron source, since the western tentacle, and to some degree the eastern, establishes a bridge of emission to this source.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PDF J010955.5 455552 as IC 1633, the cluster cD galaxy, with nuclear radio contours, a possible radio lobe, and an evolved absorption-line spectrum.

quotes.quote: PDF J010955.5 455552: This is IC 1633, the cluster cD galaxy. The radio contours are centred on the optical nucleus. An extension to the north-east is possible evidence of a radio lobe. The spectrum shows no emission features, and has absorption lines characteristic of an evolved stellar population.

sources.summary: The paper notes IC1633's published surface-brightness and HST imaging, central radial-velocity substructure, and SSP parameters that can be compared with earlier measurements.

quotes.quote: IC1633 The surface brightness profile of this galaxy was published by Schombert (1986), and HST imaging by Laine et al. \ (2003). The radial velocity profile reveals some evidence for substructure at the centre of this galaxy (Paper 1). The SSP equivalent parameters derived for the central 1 8 R _ e in this study ( (age) = 0.935 0.187; [ E/Fe ] = 0.440 0.039; [ Z/H ] = 0.380 0.061) could be compared with those presented in T05 for the central 1 10 R _ e ( (age) = 0.716 0.308; [ E/Fe ] = 0.366 0.024; [ Z/H ] = 0.563 0.057).

gmw_00003885.name: PDF J011018.0 455556
gmw_00003885.overview: PDF J011018.0 455556 is an S0 galaxy that features extended radio contours centered on its optical nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. The outer regions of the galaxy display discernible structure, possibly due to dust, while its spectrum reveals an evolved stellar population with no identifiable emission features [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J011018.0 455556 as an S0 galaxy with extended nuclear radio contours, possible dusty structure, and an evolved stellar population without identifiable emission features.

quotes.quote: PDF J011018.0 455556: This S0 galaxy has extended radio contours centred on the optical nucleus. The outer parts of the optical object have discernible structure, perhaps due to dust.
The spectrum shows no identifiable emission features, and evolved stellar population absorption features are clearly detected.

gmw_00003886.name: PDF J011019.4 455113
gmw_00003886.overview: PDF J011019.4 455113 is a lenticular galaxy that represents the strongest radio source in its sample [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its radio contours are significantly displaced from the optical nucleus, which may be due to localized disk star formation, a radio lobe, or a radio supernova [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J011019.4 455113 as the sample's strongest radio source, with radio contours displaced from the optical nucleus and several possible origins considered.

quotes.quote: PDF J011019.4 455113: This S0 galaxy is the strongest radio source in the sample. The radio contours are displaced significantly from the optical nucleus. No spectrum is available. The radio source could be (1) a coincidentally located background source; (2) nuclear emission displaced due to astrometric errors; (3) strong, localised disk star formation; (4) a radio lobe; or (5) a radio supernova associated with the galaxy. Case (1) is unlikely, as there is only a 0.2 chance of an accidental coincidence between optical and radio sources of this magnitude.

gmw_00003887.name: PDF J011029.4 461027
gmw_00003887.overview: PDF J011029.4 461027 is a face-on flocculent spiral galaxy classified as Sb(p) [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. It features optical emission knots near its nucleus and strong H II regions, with star formation likely driving its radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J011029.4 461027 as a flocculent Sb(p) spiral with nuclear optical knots, H II-region emission, and radio emission likely powered by star formation.

quotes.quote: PDF J011029.4 461027: Dressler's classification is Sb(p). This face-on flocculent spiral shows knots of optical emission towards the nucleus. The radio contours are extended but centred on the optical nucleus. The spectrum has emission lines which indicate the galaxy has strong H ii regions. The IRAS 60 flux density is 0.455 Jy, giving S_ 60 /S_ 1.4 GHz =96.0 , consistent with the well-established radio/FIR correlation for spiral galaxies.
Star formation is the likely cause of the radio emission in this galaxy.

gmw_00003888.name: PDF J011047.2 454701
gmw_00003888.overview: PDF J011047.2 454701 is an S0 lenticular galaxy that features six apparent optical dwarf companion galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413]. Its spectrum indicates an evolved stellar population, while displaced radio contours suggest the presence of an active galactic nucleus radio lobe [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: The paper describes PDF J011047.2 454701 as an S0 galaxy with dwarf companions, displaced radio contours, an evolved spectrum, and a possible AGN radio lobe.

quotes.quote: PDF J011047.2 454701: This S0 galaxy has six apparent dwarf companions in the optical image. The radio contours are displaced from the optical nucleus, and two apparent companions show radio emission. The spectrum shows an evolved stellar population and no hint of emission. This suggests that the offset radio source could be the radio lobe of an AGN. This group of radio and optical sources is quite unusual and invites further study.
